Name: Bijan Robinson
Outlook: While other fantasy RBs stole the headlines last year, Bijan quietly put together an insanely efficient sophomore season en route to finishing as the RB4. He posted the highest rush success rate (60.2%) ever for a RB with 250+ carries. Since 2007, only two RBs had 17+ fantasy points in 10 of their final 11 games: 2024 Bijan and 2019 CMC. The Falcons followed through on their commitment to increase his workload including the 3rd most red zone touches among RBs. Bijan enters his age 23 season checking all the boxes you want for elite fantasy performers: young, talented, proven, and on an ascending offense. He might have the highest floor of any fantasy RB so if you feel more comfortable taking him over other backs at the beginning of the first, we get it.

Name: Saquon Barkley
Outlook: It was a magical season behind that Philly offensive line. He registered the most yards after contact of any RB since the NFL started tracking that data (2016) and an unreal seven runs of 40+ yards. For perspective, that’s more than Todd Gurley had in his entire career. He had 12 TDs of 10+ yards as an Eagle last year, counting regular season and playoffs. For context, he had 13 total rushing TDs of 10+ yards in six seasons as a Giant. The offense is elite, his workload is secure, and any Jalen Hurts tush-related worries have been pushed aside. Repeating as fantasy’s #1 RB is really, really tough, but don’t overthink this thing: he is elite and deserving of a top-5 fantasy pick.

Name: Christian McCaffrey
Outlook: There are moments in life that require a true leap of faith. Do you dare tango with the most tantalizing force in fantasy football yet again in 2025? CMC enters his age-29 season facing a similar storyline: crushing the souls of fantasy managers. Sometimes it is on the field as he’s averaged 21 opportunities and 115 total yards in 2.5 years as a 49er; however, 2024 reminded us of the pain of years gone by as he played in just four games after being selected as the 1.01. The draft cost is much lower this year, but we are split on his ability to return to elite top-3 status. Regardless, his workload is unrivaled among fantasy RBs on a 49ers team projected for double-digit wins. If you are still recovering and tending to your wounds, we get it. What is your risk tolerance in the first round of your draft, and are you willing to miss out on a discount from arguably the most impactful fantasy RB of this generation?

Name: Jahmyr Gibbs
Outlook: He is the most explosive back in the NFL with a league-leading 10% of his carries gaining 15+ yards. His 20(!) total TDs on 302 touches are akin to prime Jamaal Charles as a game-breaking runner who also finds paydirt at such a high rate. Despite splitting time with David Montgomery, Gibbs was at his best against top-half defenses, averaging 20.9 fantasy points per game in those contests. It helps that Detroit’s #1 scoring offense continues to operate as a juggernaut, so projecting him for at least 12+ total TDs feels almost too low. In terms of first round picks, Gibbs is one of the safest for 2025.

Name: Derrick Henry
Outlook: Any age worries were quickly dismissed as Henry totaled over 2,000 scrimmage yards and 18 total TDs as a Raven in his ninth season in the NFL. Henry’s splits have always closely followed team wins and losses as he’s averaged nearly 10 more fantasy points per game in wins (19.2 vs. 9.8) in his career. While his minimal receiving work does raise concern, we’ve been down this road with the Yeti for years. If Baltimore’s projected team win total (10.5) and the offensive output continues to operate as a top-10 scoring unit, Henry should once again be in range as a top-5 fantasy back.

Name: Ashton Jeanty
Outlook: Let’s be clear: Jeanty checks nearly every box as an RB prospect. Beyond the 29 rushing TDs last year as a Heisman runner-up, he also is one of three 1st round RBs to see 20+ of their team’s receiving yardage in a single season (2023), joining some guys named Christian McCaffrey and Jahmyr Gibbs. The Raiders certainly were smitten, taking him higher than any RB in the NFL draft since Saquon Barkley in 2018. If you possess this type of draft capital (6th overall) and elite college production at this young of an age (21 years old), you crush for fantasy. Expect a mammoth workload on a much-improved Raiders team. We have him as a fringe 1st round pick in redraft, but certainly understand if you want to call your shot ahead of other established backs.

Name: De'Von Achane
Outlook: It was a tale of two seasons for Achane, and it came down to one simple question: was Tua playing? He was the RB3 in fantasy points per game (19.5) with the left-handed QB, averaging 6.5 targets per game and targeted on a whopping 29 percent of his routes. However, in the six games without him, that rate dropped to a measly 12 percent, and his fantasy points per game (7.7) suffered a massive hit. He’ll never be your prototypical fantasy RB, but after leading all RBs in receptions and 1st read targets, we don’t care! Consider him a solid RB1 with the type of upside to “win you a week”.

Name: Bucky Irving
Outlook: It took a moment for things to shift, but Bucky took his true Winter Soldier form from Week 10 until “the end of the line”. He was the RB7 in the second half of the season, averaging 17.9 fantasy points per game, morphing into a true workhorse with 30% of Tampa Bay’s total rush attempts and targets. Among all RBs with 75+ carries, he ranked 5th in yards per carry (5.42), 7th in rush success rate, 8th in explosive rush rate, and 3rd in forced missed tackle rate. While Rachaad White will continue to share the workload, Bucky looks like a surefire RB1 in our projections. Perhaps some of his efficiency numbers take a dip in Year 2, but our research shows that Year 1 breakouts like his translate into sustained fantasy success.

Name: Chase Brown
Outlook: It took a Zack Moss injury to get him started, but from Week 9 on, he was the RB5(!) in fantasy. Only Saquon saw more total opportunities per game (24.4) in that span as the Bengals loaded him up, averaging 86% of the team’s snaps. He received a major fantasy bump as a pass-catcher (54/360/4), but as a receiver, the efficiency was middling, ranking 61st out of 67 qualifying RBs in yards after catch per reception. As the lead back on one of the league’s most explosive offenses, it’s hard not to envision a repeat fringe top-10 season out of Brown after the team failed to devote any significant draft capital to the RB position.

Name: Josh Jacobs
Outlook: Think back to the discussion last year around Josh Jacobs: would he be used like a true workhorse, given Matt LeFleur’s history of using a committee approach? We got our answer as Jacobs was fifth in the NFL in total touches, finishing as the RB5 in fantasy football. He scored in every game from Week 11 on with 12 total TDs in his final eight games played. This is a good offense that should have scoring opportunities, but if the TD production swings back to Jordan Love and the passing game a little bit, Jacobs could have a really tough time replicating his career-best 16 total TDs and NFL-best 14 rushing TDs inside the 10-yard line. Consider us slightly lower than where he is being drafted as more of a fringe RB1 for your roster.

Name: Kyren Williams
Outlook: Since bursting onto the scene in 2023, Kyren has done everything asked of him with 31 total TDs in his last 28 games played, a feat only Jahmyr Gibbs can match. He led the NFL in red zone touches and rushing TDs inside the five last year as the Rams “Plan A” when they got in close. What he lacks in pure explosiveness, he makes up for in raw volume. Sean McVay loves this guy, and while there was off-season talk of working in “more guys” in the backfield, we’ll believe it when we see it! He comes slightly at a discount this year as a back-end RB1 on a Rams team projected for double-digit wins.

Name: Jonathan Taylor
Outlook: Remember that end-of-year surge? His 520 rushing yards over the final three games were the fifth-most yards of all time! In fact, 38% of his total fantasy points a year ago came in those final three games. Taylor has always been an electric runner of the football who’s capable of huge games, but after some ankle injuries in recent years, it’s definitely fair to question if he’s the same back he was a few years ago when he finished as the RB1 overall for fantasy. Last season, among 46 qualified backs that logged 100+ carries, Taylor ranked 42nd in forced missed tackle rate and 44th in yards after contact per attempt. His pass-catching work is an afterthought, so his fantasy prospects for 2025 come down to what you believe about the Colts offense. He is an RB1, but we doubt if the top-5 ceiling is still prevalent.

Name: Alvin Kamara
Outlook: Can’t stop! Won’t stop! Kamara continues to defy all age-related doubts after leading all RBs in expected fantasy points per game and target share as a 29-year-old. Despite having a weird scoreless streak from Weeks 7 to 14, he still finished as a top-10 fantasy RB on the back of 89 targets and over 500 receiving yards. We know what he is: a PPR floor play stuck on a rebuilding Saints offense. We don’t expect a ton to change under new HC Kellen Moore as Kamara profiles as the team’s most dependable pass-catcher alongside Chris Olave. Even with a rookie QB under center, we find ourselves drafting Kamara as a solid RB2. Age is just a number, and so is the fact that a target is worth over two times as much as a carry in fantasy football.

Name: James Conner
Outlook: Coming off back-to-back 1,000-yard seasons at the age of 30, how many fantasy lives does it feel like this guy has? The workload entrusted to him in Arizona is rarified air as he was one of only 3 RBs with average 15+ carries and 3+ receptions per game joining Alvin Kamara and Bijan Robinson. He’s a tackle-breaking machine and despite the age concerns, he is the engine of this Cardinals offense. Conner is perennially disrespected in ADP but he continues to function as a high-end RB2.
